coredata.dc:title: Biological matrix-derived carbon quantum dots: Highly selective detection of tetracyclines
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Ximing
Wang, Lishuang
Ye, Heng
Zhao, Hanqing
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: As a kind of abundant resource, the development of biomass has been drawn quite more curiosity to the present research community. In this study, a facile, environment friendly, and repeatable one-step hydrothermal synthesis approach of CDs (carbon dots) by using carp roe as raw material was established. The CDs prepared from carp roe did not require any surface modification, besides, it has the properties such as low toxicity, fantastic water solubility and environmental friendliness. The average particle size of the prepared CDs was about 7.60 nm, and the maximum excitation/emission wavelength was 365/443 nm. In order to verify the low toxicity of CDs, two kinds of cells HCT-116 (human colon cancer cell) and MC (mouse cardiomyocytes) were used to investigate the cytotoxicity of CDs. Owing to the inner filter effect (IFE), the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched in the presence of tetracycline antibiotics (TCs), realizing the detection of tetracycline, chlortetracycline, oxytetracycline and other tetracyclines, which has high accuracy and selectivity, providing the foundation of TCs quantification. The value of fluorescence intensity has a linear relationship with the concentration of TCs in the range of 0.1–50 µM, and the detection limit was 41.7 nM. It was suitable to be applied in the detection of tetracycline in river water, serum and milk with good recovery rate (98.6∼104.5%), demonstrating its potential and broad application prospects in detecting TCs in complex matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Biomass
Carbon dots
TCs detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TCs by the CDs
Detection of TCs in real sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University ( ZQN2016011 ), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department ( 2020LZD02 ), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis and application of CDs.

(a) TEM images of CDs with different magnification (Insert in (a) was size distribution (statistics: 50 measurements from TEM image) of CDs; (b) HRTEM images of CDs; (c) XRD patterns of the CDs.; (d) XPS survey spectrum of CDs; (e) High-resolution C1s peaks and fitting curves; (f) High-resolution N1s peaks and fitting curves; (g) High-resolution O1s peaks and fitting curves.

(a) UV–vis spectra of CDs (black) and, excitation (red) and emission spectra (blue) of CDs (Insert in (a) was the image of CDs under sunlight and fluorescence) of CDs; (b) Emission spectra of CDs for varying excitation wavelengths; (c) FT-IR spectra of CDs.
(a) Fluorescence intensity of CDs under different pH conditions; (b) Fluorescence intensity of CDs under different ionic strength. (n = 3).
(a) Influence of different ions on the fluorescence of CDs; (b) Influence of different antibiotics on the fluorescence of CDs.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of TC. (Inset: The left side shows the situation without TC, and the right side shows the situation with TC); (b) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of CDs with various concentrations of TC; (c) Fluorescent spectra of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of CTC; (d) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of CDs with various concentrations of CTC; (e) Fluorescent spectra of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of OTC; (f) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of CDs with various concentrations of OTC. (n = 3).

(a) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs; (b) Schematic diagram of internal filtration effect, UV–vis spectra of TC (black) and, excitation (red) and emission spectra (blue) of CDs (The diagonally shaded part indicates the overlap between the UV–vis spectra of TC and the excitation spectrum of CDs).

(a) Cell viability of the HCT-116 and MC after incubation with the photoluminescent CDs for 60 h. (b) Cell image of the HCT-116 incubate with the photoluminescent CDs at 0 h; (c) Cell image of the HCT-116 after incubation with the photoluminescent CDs for 60 h; (d) Cell image of the HMC incubate with the photoluminescent CDs at 0 h; (e) Cell image of the HMC after incubation with the photoluminescent CDs for 60 h. (n = 3).
Comparison of different Tetracyclines detection methods.
Recoveries of TC supplemented in real samples detected by the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Biological matrix-derived carbon quantum dots: Highly selective detection of tetracyclines

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning 110016, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a kind of abundant resource, the development of biomass has been drawn quite more curiosity to the present research community. In this study, a facile, environment friendly, and repeatable one-step hydrothermal synthesis approach of CDs (carbon dots) by using carp roe as raw material was established. The CDs prepared from carp roe did not require any surface modification, besides, it has the properties such as low toxicity, fantastic water solubility and environmental friendliness. The average particle size of the prepared CDs was about 7.60 nm, and the maximum excitation/emission wavelength was 365/443 nm. In order to verify the low toxicity of CDs, two kinds of cells HCT-116 (human colon cancer cell) and MC (mouse cardiomyocytes) were used to investigate the cytotoxicity of CDs. Owing to the inner filter effect (IFE), the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched in the presence of tetracycline antibiotics (TCs), realizing the detection of tetracycline, chlortetracycline, oxytetracycline and other tetracyclines, which has high accuracy and selectivity, providing the foundation of TCs quantification. The value of fluorescence intensity has a linear relationship with the concentration of TCs in the range of 0.1–50 µM, and the detection limit was 41.7 nM. It was suitable to be applied in the detection of tetracycline in river water, serum and milk with good recovery rate (98.6∼104.5%), demonstrating its potential and broad application prospects in detecting TCs in complex matrix.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging technology, nanotechnology produce an astonishing effect in many fields, for example, medicine, thermology, optics, electricity, and chemistry. As an emerging fluorescent carbon material, carbon dots had been caught remarkable attention since 2004 [1]. Thanks to the good photoluminescence property, carbon dots (CDs) has become a new generation of fluorescent carbon-based nano materials. The photoluminescence of CDs is mainly reflected in their surface defects, serving as surface energy traps, hosting the radiative recombination of excitons. In addition to the captivating fluorescent properties, CDs have drawn widespread attention due to possess numerously superior characteristic, for instance, fantastic optical properties, excellent water solubility, unique biocompatibility and non-toxic to the environment [2]. As a result of the excellent optical properties, CDs has been widely used in ion detection [3–6], organic matter detection [7–9], bioimaging [10], photocatalysis [11], photo electrochemistry [12] and biosensor [13] in the past decades. Owing to the wide application of CDs, quantities of studies have been produced on the synthesis of CDs. Many researchers have prepared CDs based on different chemical carbon sources as precursors in the past, for instance amino acids or proteins. Over the past several years, in order to reach the purpose of cheap, green and sustainable, it is reported that there have been numerous natural biomass being used as a carbon source for the synthesis of CDs to take the place of chemical carbon sources, including garlic [14], hawthorn [15], crab shell [16], fingernails [17], fish scales [18], lemon juice [19], and etc. [20–22].

A method for rapid analysis of tetracycline antibiotics by using fluorescence technology has been developed in this article. This technique is not only unsophisticated in synthesis method, quick in response, low in cost, and high in accuracy, but more importantly, environment friendly. Herein, one pot solvothermal method was used to synthesize CDs using carp roe, which is a kind of commonly biomass containing rich fatty acids and proteins. Through a series of dehydration and carbonization processes [28], the carbon precursor is heated and cracked to synthesize CDs under high temperature and high pressure. Owing to the inner filter effect (IFE), the fluorescence intensity of CDs is substantially quenched in the presence of tetracyclines, setting up the foundation of tetracyclines quantification (Fig. 2 ). Compared with other conventional carbon dots in Table 1, the new fluorescent sensing material synthesized by this method realizes the detection of three tetracycline compounds successfully, and the analytical characteristics such as sensitivity and linear range are better than the proposed detection technique. More importantly, the use of biomass materials as a precursor without any surface modification is more environment friendly than traditional carbon dots. This method has been successfully applied to the practical determination of tetracycline in complex matrix (river water, milk and serum). Altogether, this work combined a quick detection with harmless raw materials, extending a new prospect for the preparation of fluorescent materials and antibiotics detection.

The carp was obtained from the local market (Shenyang, China). Ionic solutions including BaCl2, NH4Cl, CaCl2, CdCl2, Mg (NO3)2, MnCl2, NiSO4, PbCl2, ZnCl2 were purchased from Tianjin Hengxing chemical reagent manufacturing Co., Ltd （Tianjin, China). And various types of antibiotics, for instance tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), gentamicin (GEN), ciprofloxacin (CIP), cefazolin (CEZ), were acquired from Chengdu Deste Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). The purchased reagents are all analytical grade, allocation by deionized water, which can be used without further purification.

The excitation and emission spectra were implemented on F97 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd, China). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CDs was surveyed by using Thermo Fisher EscaLab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a D8-Focus X-ray diffractometer (Brook, German). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were performed by using UV-5500 PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Metash Instrument Co., Ltd, China). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were gained by using JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were measured by using Thermo Fisher Nicolet IS50 FT-IR spectrometer. The pH values were obtained by PHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd, China).

Firstly, the fish roe taken out was dried in a drying oven and smashed into powder. Dispersing 1.0 g of roe powder in 50 ml of deionized water, then heat at 200 °C for 12 h in a teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave [31]. Natural cooling the autoclave afterwards, the dispersion was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was extracted. After filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous, the obtained supernatant was transferred into a dialysis bag with molecular weight of 500 Da, dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h. Finally, the brown products were acquired by freeze- drying, which was dispersed by deionized water to prepare a transparent suspension with a concentration of 0.1 mg/ml for subsequent experiments.

Quantum yield (QY) is one of the important factors to analyze the characteristics of luminescent materials. Compared with the relative quantum yield, the determination of absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (Abs-QY) can reduce experimental system errors and accidental errors. ABS-QY of CDs was determined by integrating sphere and fluorescence spectroscopy. In this report, we used a technique for the quantitative measurement of the Abs-QY of CDs basing on the steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer and an integrating sphere.

The Abs-QY is defined as Eq. (1):
(1) Abs-QY = Lsample ÷ (Ereference-Esample)
where Abs-QY is the quantum yield, Lsample is the emission intensity of the sample. The Ereference is the intensity of the excitation light not absorbed by the reference sample and the Esample is the intensity of the excitation light not absorbed by the sample, respectively [32].

TC was selected as a representative researching model for TCs. Another important parameter to evaluate the performance of CDs is selectivity, so the interaction behavior of various competing metal ions with CDs was assessed. By evaluating the effect of different sorts of molecules and ions on the fluorescence intensity, the selectivity and interference of CDs can be evaluated in the absence and presence of TCs. Various sorts of ions (Ba2+, NH4 +, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cl−, SO4 2− and NO3 −) and various sorts of antibiotics (tetracyclines, cephalosporins, quinolones, penicillins, aminoglycosides, chloramphenicols and macrolides) with a concentration of 5 mM were selected for the selectivity study. All the measurements were recorded under normal conditions at a fluorescence excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

River water was collected from Bo Song Lake (Benxi, China) while the milk samples were purchased in a local convenience store and the serum was gathered from Shenyang army general hospital. The primeval river water samples need to be pretreated by centrifuge (4200r/min, 10 min) and filtered the sample through a 0.22 µm membrane to eliminate suspended particles. The milk samples were treated by acetonitrile to remove proteins, ultrasonicated the mixture suspension for 15 min. After that, the solution was centrifuged at 4200 rpm for 10 min. Gathering the supernatant and then filtering it through a microporous membrane with a pore size of 0.22 µm. The serum samples were pre-stored in a deep refrigerator before use, diluted it with deionized water till its concentration becomes one fiftieth of the original value. Three concentrations of tetracycline (2, 10 and 20 µM) were added into the above samples, and their contents were determined to survey TC. The contents of TC in authentic complex samples were surveyed by fluorescence spectroscopy.

In order to assess the toxicity of carbon dots, we conducted cytotoxicity experiments with reference to previously reported techniques [33]. The cytotoxicity and bio-compatibility of carbon dots were detected by employing CCK-8 colorimetric assay. In this work, we gauged the survival rate of mouse cardiomyocytes (MC) and HCT116 in the presence of carbon dots. MC and HCT-116 were incubated for 48 h in dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) at the constant temperature of 37 °C, 5% CO2. Firstly, infusing MC and HCT116 on Costar 96-well tissue culture clusters with quantity (5000–10,000 for each well), and then cultivate for 24 h. Ultra-pure water was used as a solvent to dilute the carbon dots to a series of concentrations, adding 100 μl of carbon dots solution for different concentrations (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 µg/ml), incubating for 12, 24, 48, and 60 h, respectively. Each sample should be repeated three times for parallel and the control groups were cells without adding carbon dots. 10 µl CCK-8 solution was added into each well, which was placed in an incubator and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. Then softly aspirating the medium from each well and dissolving formazan before shaking it on constant temperature shaker at 85 rpm for 15 min. Finally, absorbance of each well should be measured in a microplate reader at 450 nm, and calculating the cell survival rate according to the following equation:
cell viability (%) = [(As-Ab)/(Ac-Ab)] × 100%
where As is the absorbance of tested compound which contains absorbance of containing cell, culture medium, CCK-8 and tested compound, Ab and Ac is the culture medium and the absorbance without tested compound, respectively.

TEM, QY and XPS were used to characterize the morphology, QY and elemental composition of CDs. The particle morphology and size distribution of CDs were illustrated by TEM images (Fig. 3 a). The shape of CDs is roughly spherical and uniform in size, which is well dispersed with a diameter range of 6.04 to 9.42 nm. By evaluating 50 random particles, an average particle size of 7.60 nm can be obtained. The nanoparticles have high crystallinity with a lattice parameter of 0.24 nm, which corresponds to graphitic lattice [34] (Fig. 3b). Fig. 3c shows the X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of CDs. The pattern clearly showed an intense diffraction peak approximately 2θ at 23.5°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite. In summary, the above results reveal that the as-prepared CDs are highly crystalline and graphitic materials. At the meanwhile, we measured the QY of CDs was 13.4%, having a higher QY comparing with other CDs that are modified without other chemical reagents. We used XPS to further obtain information on the elemental composition and chemical bonds of CDs. In Fig. 3d, a full range XPS scan was used to perform elemental analysis. Carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen are represented by three peaks of 284.69, 399.58, and 530.96 eV, and the content of elemental composition is 66 %, 13.98 %, and 20.02 %. From the spectrum shown in Fig. 3d, CDs are only composed of C, N and O without other element, which indicates the carbon skeleton of CDs is modified by nitrogen and oxygen groups. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 3e), three characteristic peaks at 284.2, 285.1 and 287.7 eV were recorded, exhibiting the presence of C–C/C=C, C-N, C=O in CDs [10,35,36]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s showed the main peaks of C-N-C and N–H, corresponding to 399.3 and 400.1 eV, respectively [37,38] (Fig. 3f). Meanwhile, in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3g), two peaks were observed at 530.6 and 531.5 eV, which were related to the HO-C=O and C=O groups [39,40]. From the study, showing the surface of CDs is existing a large number of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups which gives CDs with high hydrophilicity leading wonderful stable dispersibility in aqueous solutions.

The optical performance of CDS was characterized by fluorescence spectrophotometer, UV–vis and FT-IR. The CDs dissolved in deionized water presents a clear and transparent light-yellow light under sunlight, while under ultraviolet light (λ = 365 nm) it presents a strong blue luminescence pattern (Fig. 4 a inset). As shown in Fig. 4a, CDs has an absorption peak near 255 nm in the UV–vis spectrum, which can be ascribable to the n-π* transition of the aromatic C=O bond, and these transitions cannot be observed by fluorescence signal. Demonstrating by spectrum in Fig. 4b, the intensity and peak position of fluorescence emission are determined by the excitation wavelength, so the fluorescence researches were conducted under different excitation wavelengths. The emission wavelength increases as the excitation wavelength increases. As the excitation wavelength varied from 300 to 460 nm, the emission maxima gradually shifts to longer wavelength, a red shift occurs, and the intensity peak decreased. It is finally found that the best excitation wavelength is 365 nm, while the maximum emission wavelength of CDs irradiated by the optimal excitation wavelength is 443 nm. Therefore, 365 nm was chosen as the excitation wavelength for the subsequent experiments.

In order to identify the functional groups of CDs, the surface of CDs was evaluated by FT-IR (Fig. 4c). The absorption peak at 3450 cm−1 was designated as the stretching vibration peak of the N–H bond [41]. The two strong absorption bands around 1650 cm−1 were finely matched with the vibration of the C=O bond and the N–H bond in the amide group [29]. The two absorption peaks overlap each other, resulting in the interference between the two peaks. The characteristic peak at 1385 cm−1 was assigned as the stretching vibration of the C-N bond in the amide group [42]. The peak present at 1050 cm−1 indicated for the presence of C-O bond stretching vibration [43]. This series of peaks can be attributed to the presence of amide groups. Moreover, the strong peaks at 3200 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the O–H bond [43]. The absorption peak at about 2900 cm−1 corresponding to the hydrocarbon stretching vibration of the methylene group. The peak centered at 1399 cm−1 was accounted for relating to the additional peak of methylene. In general, oxygen-containing groups, nitrogen-containing groups and unsaturated carbon structures were enriched on the surface of CDs, which can enhance the hydrophilicity of CDs.

Stability studies were performed to discussed the stability of CDs under different pH, ionic strength, and UV light time by means of the fluorescence intensity. For the purpose of verifying the impact of pH to the intensity, the CDs solution were adjusted to aqueous solutions of different pH (1–13). From the experiments, the fluorescence intensity was observed on different pH medium to illustrate the stability (Fig. 5 a). The pH is from 3 to 10, its value hardly changing, when at extraordinary low or high pH, it gets a low fluorescence intensity. The observable fact could be explained that the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs would be protonated or deprotonated, resulting in a decrease of fluorescence intensity. When the pH is at the minimal and maximum condition, the fluorescence intensity reduces, but it does not at a moderate pH [44]. Meanwhile, different environments were tested with different ion intensity and light irradiation time. Various concentrations of the NaCl were added to simulated different ionic strength environments. The fluorescence intensity was stable under different ionic strength, indicating the superb tolerance of CDs to high ionic strength condition (Fig. 5b). The CDs solution was irradiated under ultraviolet light for 90 min for measuring the influence of irradiation time. The result proved that the fluorescence intensity of CDs after 90 min of irradiation can still maintain more than 90 % of the original intensity, proving that CDs have excellent fluorescence stability. The above properties demonstrate that CDs derived from carp roe have enormous stability under strict conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs by the CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The content of total TCs was determined using CDs in the samples to assess whether the total content is the sum of different kinds of TCs. The samples were gauged on the basis of the anterior method [30]. Performing fluorescence intensity detection to single TCs samples (TC, CTC and OTC each 30 μM), binary TCs samples (TC + CTC, TC + OTC and CTC + OTC each 15 µM), ternary TCs samples (TC + CTC + OTC each 10 µM), respectively. It is found that the fluorescence intensity of single TCs samples was identical with TCs existed at different mixtures under the same concentration conditions. The outcome demonstrated that CDs can be practically applied to detect total TCs, having extraordinary application prospects.

The mechanism of CDs detecting TC was discussed by this experiment. TC was used as a model compound, measuring the fluorescence decay curve when TC was present or not present. In order to determine the type of quenching of CDs by TC, the fluorescence lifetime was measured. As shown in Fig. 8 , comparing the fluorescence lifetime before and after adding TC, the fluorescence lifetime was 3.56 ns without changing, indicating that the decrease aroused by TC in fluorescence intensity is attributed to static quenching rather than dynamic quenching. To further explore the mechanism of static quenching, the UV–vis spectra of TC were prepared, discovering that TC had strong ultraviolet absorption peaks at 276 nm and 357 nm, which had sturdy overlaps with the excitation spectra of fluorescent probes (Fig. 8b). Thus, TCs would absorb the excitation light of the probe, thereby reducing its fluorescence intensity without changing fluorescence lifetime. According to the results above, it was assumed that the fluorescence quenching mechanism between CDs and TCs could be due to an inner filter effect (IFE) [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of exploring the applicability of the sensor in real samples, river water, milk and serum samples were detected by CDs. The feasibility of the analytical approach was assessed via the standard addition method to detect TCs residues in real samples. The TCs was not detected in river water, milk or serum samples. In addition, three different concentrations of TC (2 µM, 10 µM, 20 µM) was added to the three samples, respective. As shown in the Table 2 below, the average recovery rate of standard samples ranged from 98.6% to 104.5% (Table 2), and the reproducibility （RSD%) value was lower than 2.62 % (n = 3). These values illustrate that CDs have good accuracy, and it is feasible to detect TCs in actual samples. There is no obvious interference matrix, and TCs can be monitored quickly, having a tremendous application in food detection prospect.

In the presence of CDs, the survival rates of MC and HCT-116 were gauged to assess the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility. Firstly, the CCK-8 colorimetric method was used to detect the cytotoxic effects of CDs on MC and HCT-116. On the basis of the previous experimental method, survival rate of cells was examined after exposing to CDs solutions of different concentrations (12.5∼200 μg/ml). As shown in Fig. 9 , after 60 h of incubation, the cell survival rate was higher than 90 %, even at the highest concentration of CDs. The result demonstrated that no significant difference was observed by the student-t test between CDs-treated samples with the control group. Low toxicity and extraordinary biocompatibility of CDs were reflected through the outcome, showing fantastically prospects in biomedical applications.

In conclusion, a facile, environment friendly, and repeatable one-step hydrothermal CDs material using carp roe as raw material was established. The CDs prepared are spherical in shape with the average particle size of 7.60 nm, which is incredibly stable, relying on the high luminescence characteristics of excitation and low cytotoxicity. Moreover, the CDs prepared from carp roe without any surface modification. In addition, the results of XPS and FT-IR demonstrated that oxygen-containing groups was contained in CDs, which is beneficial to the improvement of solubility and fluorescence properties. In the presence of TCs, due to the IFE, the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched, thereby realizing the detection of TCs such as TC, CTC, and OTC. These CDs were also tested for HCT-116 and MC cytotoxicity, which displayed quite low cytotoxicity. This study fully elaborated that carp roe is a natural raw material for the preparation of CDs, which provides a new approach for TCs detection, broadening application prospects in the fields of biosensing and biomedical imaging.

Ximing Tang: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Lishuang Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Heng Ye: Validation, Investigation. Hanqing Zhao: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Software. Longshan Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).
